text: Planning and executing a rocket launch is normally a slow process.
An astronomically expensive and challenging logistical operation.
However, last year the industry took a major leap forward.
With only 24 hours notice from the United States Space Force,
Firefly managed to integrate a payload into its alpha-rockets.
Plan its launch profile, get their rocket positioned and fueled on the launch pad,
and finally launched, shattering the previous 21 day record.
All in an effort to carve out a niche for itself in the highly competitive space launch
Marquis, which is currently dominated by SpaceX.
SpaceX is launching more into space than any other company,
thanks to reusable rockets driving down the price of launch,
but that doesn't mean there isn't space for others in the Marquis.
Being able to launch at a moment's notice is a really attractive capability.
For a commercial company, say you have a satellite ready to launch
that will generate a million dollars in revenue every month it's an orbit.
The next available launch for a right-share, which SpaceX is 12 months away.
That's 12 million dollars of last revenue.
And that's vastly more expensive than the cost of launch for most small to medium-sized satellites.
In a case like this, waiting for the cheaper launch price simply doesn't make sense.
The cost of opportunity is just too high.
Then, of course, there are the military applications, which is why the United States Space Force
contracted Firefly for the Victor Snacks mission.
We don't know what was on board that mission, but we do know why militaries are interested in getting things into space quickly.
If an enemy destroys a satellite, critical to your military operations, you want to replace it.
Quickly, if a foreign military places a satellite or even a weapon into orbit and you don't know what it is,
you want to be able to send something up to take a closer look.
Or, if World War III has broken out potentially destroys.
Firefly has focused the engineering efforts of their alpha launch vehicle with this valuable niche in mind.
And have come up with some unique designs to put them ahead of the competition.
To learn more, I spoke with Alphas, Chief Engineer, Jordi Garcia.
And one of the mission requirements was responsiveness.
What we're talking about today is how you've designed the rocket around, kind of things like the Victor Snacks mission
have just been able to launch within 24 hours.
What engineering designs can you talk about that helped towards that mission?
Yeah, so when we conceived Alpha from the beginning, we're a targeting to go quick and be able to actually load, detank and fly as fast as possible.
So for example, some of the requirements is that you want to make sure that you can load your vehicle fast and detank your vehicle fast if you need to.
Let's say you have to scrub and go the next day.
You're making sure it doesn't take you 12, 16 hours.

So there's a lot of design decisions that you need to make from the beginning, making sure that your quick disconnect lines, your field lines are sized, your van lines on a way that you can feel my target is 30 minutes.
When I started, it's like engineers in 30 minutes, I want to be able to go right on like load it.
Is there any difficulties around that of pump rates and volume is there any like hard limit you hit that.
Or is it you can just size the pump as big as you want?
No, you can because at the end what you want to get is the most mass to orbit, right?
So the problem is the bigger I make the lines and the quick disconnection the valves, the heavier it gets.
I show it's a challenge. If I could load it in 18 hours, you could go very slowly and fill it up things would be lighter.

But then you gotta make that trade. I'm having lines and valves and components that I can take to orbit and not eat too much performance, but still at the same time, be able to feel and detank quickly.
It sounds silly but one of the requirements that we have is like operators can work more than 12 hours a day, right?
So if we want to load detank, go to sleep and then go again next day because you have a scrapper and you gotta change something.
We need to build the squeeze all that timeline and make it in less than 12 hours to get grew rest and then go again the next day.
So it's something that and still now we're still polishing the more we load and detank and fly, we learn the things we can improve.
So we have a lot of design changes coming up to maybe not a lot but we have some design changes to try to improve our timing.
And the temperatures of your fuel and oxygen are different, right? Correct. Yes, yes, yes.
Our liquid oxygen is roughly minus 290. That's 300F. That's one big difference that we have from SpaceX for example that we are not super cool.
We don't actually chill the liquid oxygen together to get more performance, right? We just get it up to the vehicle and we let it bend to a saturation and at those temperatures.
Well, those pressures you achieve the temperature that you want. What are the pros and cons of that?
The pros of that is that let's say if we want to hold and wait, you can hold and wait.
If you're super cool because you have to go through a liquid nitrogen system, right? You can load and then you're more performance, right? You can remember how much.

You got an extra density from that, but you gotta go. If you wait 30 minutes, 45 minutes, don't know exactly what time it is but something like that, then you have to detank and you have to scrap for the day.
And for us as we ended to make sure that if you had, I don't know, a ship or a delay for any reason that you could still go in an hour or so.
So you're thinking that performance hip, but you're getting some more launchability from that time.

So, Firefly is balancing performance and launch availability. They have larger fuel and oxygen lines to allow them to fill their tanks quicker, but that means they carry more waste and lose some performance.
This compromise was also made when choosing propellant temperatures.
SpaceX uses super cool propellants to gain extra performance through increased density.
SpaceX needs to save some fuel to land their first stage, so every bit of extra performance is vital.
Firefly has chosen to skip the super cooling step. This brings up an interesting logistical problem with the space industry.
There are many reasons a launch may be scrubbed.
It's not uncommon for a fishing vessel to stray into the exclusion zone while a rocket is being loaded with propellant.
When this happens, the launch has to be held until the boat is cleared, but time is very much of the essence, especially for SpaceX.
Their super cool propellants can stay super cool forever.
So, they start loading propellants 35 minutes before lift off. They call this a load and go.
A delay, like a boat entering the exclusion zone, can be mission critical.
As the propellants begin to warm and lose performance, eventually enough performance is lost that they can no longer satisfy mission requirements,
and they need to drain the tanks and wait for another launch opportunity.
This delicate timing became an issue when they won the contract to launch the crew dragon to the ISS.
This load and go method had never been done for a crewed launch before.
The space shuttle loaded its propellants 7 hours before launch, which gave NASA plenty of time to find issues before the astronauts got on board,
and they had concerns about SpaceX's load and go method for crew safety.
In 2016, a Falcon 9 carrying a $2 billion Facebook satellite blew up on the launch pad during propellant load.
The metallic liner of the composite overop pressure vessel had buckled, which led to the explosion.
Had this been a crewed launch, something much more valuable than if Facebook satellite would have been lost.
But NASA eventually approved the load and go method after an extensive safety review.
While firefly isn't willingly waiting for seven hours, their warmer propellants can be continually topped off as they evaporate without losing any performance.
This elongates their launch window.
We saw this in action with the first noise of summer launch attempt on July 1st.
When ground equipment failed, the launch was simply aborted, but they simply reset the counter and timer and tried a second time shortly after, while topping off their propellants.
But fortunately, they did not find a solution for the ground equipment issue before the launch window closed,
but another opportunity was available the very next day.
There are other ways to minimize the chances of a scrub too, by reducing the complexity of the rocket and increasing reliability.
The launch of Boeing's starliner on the Atlas V rocket was scrubbed due to a faulty valve.
These are natural issues to have, but being able to prototype and test new engine components quickly gives you a massive advantage in developing a simpler and more reliable rockets.
This is one of Fireflies greatest advantages.
It's rare for space companies to have test-dands close to not only their engineers, but their manufacturing facilities too.
SpaceX's primary test site is here in McGregor, Texas.
While the majority of SpaceX's engineering staff working on the Falcon 9 and Falcon Heavy are located in California,
Blue Origin tests their engines at NASA's Marshall Space Flight Center in Huntsville, Alabama, while their engineering and manufacturing facilities are in Seattle, Washington.
Having engineering, manufacturing and testing separated slows down development and makes it harder for engineers to truly get a holistic view of their job.
Fireflies headquarters, manufacturing and testing facilities are all located just north of Austin, Texas.
If you are interested in working with them, we've just updated our jobs board with all of their open positions.
From HR to machinist to launch engineers, you can see all of their open roles on starprepolar.com.
Fireflies VP of engineering had 11 different addresses over nine years working at other new space companies.
At Firefly, she needs just one.
By joint her at one of their test-dands.
So my name is Brigid Oaks. I'm the Vice President of Engineering here. I've been with Firefly for five years.
So we're actually on our test stand one site here. So we have six different test-stands that test anything from components to full vehicle stages as well as composite structures.
So this specific test stand is for our is an engine test stand.
So we've tested reverse here, lightening here, Miranda's here.
And what I mean by those engines on the Alpha vehicle, the first stage is powered by four reaper engines. This is a reaper engine.
And then the second stage is powered by a lightning engine, a single lightning engine, which we also test on this one.
And you're testing the engine here today.
What are you testing? Like what sort of results are you looking out for?
So this specifically is one of our development engines. So we're probably we're testing every day whether it's this stand or another one.
This actually hot fire yesterday.
And we swapped some components around this development campaign.
Two things we're looking for here is improved performance on the reaper engine.
So what we look for there is different designs on the injector to increase that mixing and that sea star efficiency.
And then another thing that we're also looking at is improving our reliability on the main valve.
So there are some unique differences to the locks main valve on this engine.
To get into specifics, the video that we're making is about the fitness and the transmission.
And how big part of your focus here at Firefly is response miss like quick turn over time.
Is there any specific designs with your engines that are designed to help with response miss?
Yeah, absolutely. So I think our Alpha vehicle is actually very well suited to response of space and that market.
Most of that I think that we pride ourselves on is this top off engine cycle technology.
So Firefly is the first company that's actually loan this engine cycle to orbit.
And successfully. So what I mean by top off engine cycle.
There are a couple different open cycles that companies will use.
So GG or gas generator cycles is fairly common. So that's what Marlin runs on.
But and then there's also open expander and some other ones.
Tap off specifically as unique will be compared to the GG cycle because we don't actually have a separate combustor.
So GG cycle has its own gas generator that operates at a different mixture ratio to drive the.
The turbine. So which drives the turbo machinery and the turbo pump that takes low pressure propellants to high pressure to combust and the chamber.
We don't have that second combustor. So what we actually do is we siphon off a portion of the gas inside the main chamber and that's what actually drives our turbo pump.
And why that's inciting is a GG could be you know on a scale of an engine this size may be an additional component here.
That's highly complex has additional mass.
We were essentially replaced that with this one and a half inch line down here.
So this time I mean you can see that that's probably you know a couple pounds maybe a foot long and that replaces an entire separate combustor.
So it pulls off the gas from this chamber up here and then drives that turbine right over here.
So it's more reliable. It's it's a tune and less mass.
So that really helps us put us in a good spot for responses to some of the emissions where they really care about that reliability.
That must be a difficult.
It is so much simpler than having and like the pre-burners and everything and having those additional parts.
There's obviously engineering challenges that come with that throughout everyone would be doing it or limitations. What are they?
So I think there's probably two that come to mind. One is it is a bootstrap engine.
What I mean by that is we have to get some amount of combustion going in the main chamber to then drive the turbines and get that going.
So on the ground we can do that differently than in flight and that startup sequence is pretty sensitive. So trying to figure that out.
We were able to iterate on that pretty quickly to tune that in.
But also the thermal environment in the chamber.
And so that if you were to take that temperature gas at its maximum in that chamber to drive the turbine,
the ink will end the materials at your machinery is inside of there. They don't they're not sustainable.
So we have to figure out a way to either cool that gas that comes out or find a cooler portion in that chamber to siphon off from.
What some people have tried to do is let's inject fuel. Usually when you inject fuel you can bring the temperature down a little bit.
But that fuel is not combustion. So you're losing performance.
We don't want to do that. So we actually have a Pintal injector design here.
Bridget was on an absolute roll there, but I'm going to pause to explain some things she mentioned in more detail.
Since not everyone in the audience are rocket experts, namely around engine cycles and injectors.
As Bridget explained, firefly is the first company to successfully fly a top off engine cycle to orbit.
A rocket's engine cycle is simply how it gets propellant from the fuel and oxidizer tank to the combustion chamber.
At its simplest, this could just be pressure fed, where the fuel and oxidizer are held at high pressure, which forces them into the combustion chamber when valves are opened.
But this limits performance. We need to feed fuel and oxidizer at higher volumes and pressures to increase thrust and efficiency.
The gas generator cycle of SpaceX's Merlin engine partially burns the propellant in a separate combustor and feeds the exhaust gases into a turbine, which spins and pumps the propellants.
However, this is wasteful. As the products of the combustor are simply dumped overboard, something SpaceX is fixing with starships Raptor engine, which is a full flow staged combustion engine cycle.
The Raptor engine runs all of its fuel and oxidizer through pre-burners, with the fuel turbo pump running a fuel rich mixture and the liquid oxygen turbo pump running an oxygen rich mixture.
The excess fuel and oxygen from each side then combine in the combustion chamber in their gas-heast state where combustion is completed.
This is a revolutionary engine, the first of its kind, but look at all the parts required to make that happen.
Two sets of pre-burners and turbo pumps and all the plumbing that connects them.
Fireflies' reaver engine simply takes hot-expanding gas directly from the combustion chamber and feeds it into a turbine to power the turbo pump.
Bridget explained that they found a cool spot in the combustion chamber to extract this gas from, so the gas doesn't melt the turbine.
But she didn't tell us where. However, we can make an educated guess based on what we know about pentel injectors.
Injectors are the part of the rocket engine that introduces and mixes the fuel and oxidizer in the combustion chamber.
It's not as simple as just spraying the fuel and oxidizer into the chamber and helping for the best.
We want to distribute the fuel and oxidizer steadily and evenly to maximize fuel efficiency and to avoid combustion instability.
We also want to atomize the propellants into the tiniest droplets possible to maximize the surface area for combustion to occur.
Pintel injectors are much simpler and cheaper than the injectors used during the Saturn V program.
The injector of the Saturn V's F1 engine was massive and it needed 2832 holes precisely machined into its face.
When 1,028 for the oxidizer and 1,04 for the fuel, with each of them forming jets of oxidizer and fuel that collided to mix and atomize them.
This was an expensive and complicated injector that was plagued by combustion in the facilities.
Combusting instabilities in rocket engines are basically just oscillations that increase in magnitude when he's released from the combustion, amplifies pressure waves and structural vibrations.
Basically, the engine hits a harmonic and vibrates.
This can affect performance or destroy the engine completely, which happened to the Saturn V's F1 engine during development multiple times.
These wedges on the injector plate were actually introduced to break up those waves and prevent resonant pressure waves from building up and sustaining themselves.
This plate was heavy and expensive to manufacture.
Now, compare that to the Pintel injector used by SpaceX's Merlin and Firefly's Revered Engines, both need just one injector for the entire engine.
The total injectors have two concentric tubes that run the length of the injector.
Generally, the central tube contains the fuel and the outer tube contains the oxidizer.
When the fuel reaches the end of the injector, it meets the Pintel, which is shaped to redirect the fuel 90 degrees outwards,
where it collides with the oxidizer flowing perpendicularly downwards.
This is a much simpler design, reducing complexity and cost of manufacturing.
But, the Pintel injector has some added benefits for Firefly's tap-off engine cycle.
The Pintel injector's geometry creates two zones of recirculation within the combustion chamber, a central propellant rich zone that helps mix on burn fuel that would otherwise be spat,
straight out the nozzle throat and not contribute to thrust.
And, an outer fuel rich zone that helps form a cooling film around the combustion chamber walls.
It's here that Firefly taps off the combustion chamber gases to run the turbo pump.
A cooler region within the engine that won't melt the turbine blades.
This is an ingenious design that no other company has successfully flown to orbit.
It removes an entire part from their engine.
This is an engineer's dream, removing an entire component, reduces weight, reduces cost, reduces manufacturing complexity,
and one less part is one less part that can break.
However, this is obviously easier said than done.
Taking the gas to drive your turbo pump directly from the combustion chamber comes with some issues.
Yeah, it's going to ask of like taking the exhaust straight out of the combustion chamber if there's any issues with combustion instability of things moving around, but you've...
So, yeah, combustion instability isn't something that we actually see on this,
but that brings up a good point that another issue that some people may have is because our fuel RP1 is actually a hard to carbon.
The byproducts of a fuel rich mixture ratio can lead to sitting or coking.
And because this line goes directly to that turbine wheel, you get deposits on there, which then degrade, which reduces the size of the workplaces in there,
and degrade your actual performance strength flight.
So, you may be at 45,000 pounds of thrust when you launch, but the longer you're flying,
we're building up on that turbine inlet, which reduces your performance.
And so there's also some really interesting proprietary technologies that we've developed,
and also patented that help us not have that issue.
I think I know the answer to this, but are you able to talk about the details of that at all?
Not really, no.

but Firefly did take us on a tour of their tank manufacturing,
and when I walked into their composite manufacturing facility, where they build the composite pressure vessels,
I noticed something immediately.
They don't use a metallic liner in their tank.
You know, the same kind of liner that buckled on that Falcon 9 and caused a $2 billion satellite to explode.
Firefly's alpha rocket is actually the largest one.
Actually, the largest all composite liquid oxygen rocket tank in the world.
Now, I studied composites in my master's thesis, and I knew several people working on PhDs,
unexactly this subject.
Removing the liner has been the holy grail of composite overup pressure vessels for quite some time.
Linerless composite tanks weigh 20% less than traditional metal tanks.
However, while linerless liquid caracene tanks are relatively easy,
cryogenic tanks are much more difficult.
Jordey took me on a tour of Firefly's composite manufacturing facility to explain why.
The liquid oxygen, that's when we came the real time.
Why? And the reason is because when you go that cold, let's say roughly minus 300 fine height,
it's very important that when you manufacture the carbon between the carbon and the resin,
you don't have moisture getting into the system.
If you have moisture, you have cracks. When you go to a genetic, you're actually going to actually have ingress of liquid nitrogen,
for example, inside or liquid oxygen inside.
Then when it warms up by either external heat or accelerating and getting heat, you actually have gasification,
you go gas and it will explode.
That's where you crack.
To be honest, that was our biggest challenge for the first years.
It's like how the hell do we make the biggest liquid oxygen tank in the world that flies.
That's the one that we made and we broke a lot of tanks in the beginning.
We took a lot of fine tuning and now we have a secret sauce, a process, a control, a moisture, and a humidity.
Then mostly, I think if we have a very strong acceptance test plan and capabilities in-house, we take every single tank.
After we build it, we have it now figured out because we've flown already forward and built many of those.
We take it to the stand, we fill it with liquid nitrogen and actually we load it with the pressure and the compressive load that it will see.
So when we fly every time we fly, we know that every tank is good for those levels.
Because we have tested it at least 10% above the total structure load that it will see as well as the maximum pressure that it will see.
So how we like, screen every single component and we want to make sure that when we fly, there's no risk.
You make sure you test that unit to the levels that you will see while it flies.
Is there some danger of crack propagation from testing at the first time and then like the second time you actually see the failure happen?
So what we've seen usually is like when you break it, you break it at the first time.
So if you test it and again, because we tested higher levels that it will see in its life.
So this tank when it finishes, it's a wrap up, that's for not these next flight, it's for one of the upcoming ones.
If it were to fail, it would fail here.
After that, the pressures and loads that we'll see are lower.
So we've seen that we have margin.
As well as all these tanks are qualified, meaning that we took them to very aggressive levels at the beginning of the program.
And we proved that they would survive excess of those limits.
Okay, right. So I think that's why we think it's beefy enough and if you screen it and you ensure that there's no cracks, there's no errors on that build.
You talked a bit about your secret sauce too and it's obviously secret for a reason.

That's one that's very tricky when you get bigger.

That's why there's other launch vehicles that also use composites.

You don't get it to minus 300.
